hook.scenario: You copy an LLM code example from a blog post. The API call shape looks slightly different from what you see in the docs. Now there are three different ways 'on the internet' to call GPT — which one is actually current?
hook.question: What is the canonical shape of a chat completions call, and which response fields matter?

mental_model.intuition: One function call with a messages list in, one message object out. Everything else — streaming, tools, structured output — is a parameter on top of this single shape.
mental_model.description: The chat completions API is the single interface for almost all LLM work: text generation, extraction, classification, agents, RAG answer generation. It takes a list of role-tagged messages, sends them to a model, and returns the model's reply as another message object. Mastering this shape means every other feature (streaming, tools, JSON mode) is just one more parameter.

sections.body: The request has three required things: a model name, a messages list, and your API key (passed via the client). The messages list is ordered: system first (optional but standard), then alternating user/assistant turns. Every call is stateless — you re-send the full history each time. The model sees only what's in this list.
sections.recap: Request: model + messages[]. Stateless — full history re-sent every call.

sections.body: The response object has a predictable shape. choices[0].message.content is the text you want. choices[0].finish_reason tells you why generation stopped: 'stop' means the model finished naturally; 'length' means you hit max_tokens; 'tool_calls' means the model wants to call a tool. usage.total_tokens is what you were billed. Ignore everything else for 90% of use cases.
sections.recap: Response: choices[0].message.content = text. finish_reason + usage.total_tokens are the two other useful fields.

sections.body: The key optional parameters: max_tokens caps the output length (set this — uncapped outputs can be very long and expensive). temperature controls randomness (0 for factual, 0.7 for creative). n=2 gets you two independent completions for the same prompt (doubles the cost). response_format={type: 'json_object'} asks for JSON — but doesn't validate schema (more on that in the structured-output node).
sections.recap: Always set max_tokens. Temperature and response_format are the two other everyday levers.

sections.body: Error handling is non-negotiable in production. The API returns HTTP errors: 400 (bad request — your messages[] list is malformed or over the context limit), 401 (bad API key), 429 (rate limit — back off and retry), 500/503 (server error — retry with backoff). The openai SDK raises typed exceptions; catch them specifically rather than swallowing all errors.
sections.recap: Catch 400 (your bug), 429 (rate limit — retry), 500 (server — retry). Never swallow all errors.

code_snippets.title: The minimal canonical call — learn this shape cold
code_snippets.code:
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from env

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are a concise assistant.'},
        {'role': 'user',   'content': 'What is idempotency in REST APIs?'},
    ],
    max_tokens=200,    # always cap output length
    temperature=0.2,   # factual task — lean deterministic
)

text = response.choices[0].message.content
print(text)
code_snippets.explanation: This is the shape every other feature builds on. Note: OPENAI_API_KEY is read from the environment — never hardcode keys. max_tokens prevents runaway expensive completions. The response's .content is a plain string.

code_snippets.title: Inspect what you were billed and why the model stopped
code_snippets.code:
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'List 50 countries in Europe.'}],
    max_tokens=100,   # intentionally short to demo truncation
)

print(response.choices[0].finish_reason)   # 'length' — hit max_tokens, not done
print(response.usage.prompt_tokens)        # tokens in the input
print(response.usage.completion_tokens)    # tokens in the output
print(response.usage.total_tokens)         # what you're billed
code_snippets.explanation: finish_reason='length' means the model was cut off mid-answer — increase max_tokens or redesign the prompt. finish_reason='stop' means it finished naturally. Always log usage.total_tokens in production to detect runaway costs.

code_snippets.title: Production wrapper: error handling + basic retry
code_snippets.explanation: Exponential backoff on 429 and 5xx is standard practice. Never retry 4xx (except 429) — those are client errors that won't fix themselves. The openai SDK exposes typed exception classes so you can target specific errors without catching everything.

common_mistakes.title: Not setting max_tokens
common_mistakes.explanation: Without max_tokens the model can generate a very long response, which is slow and expensive. For tasks where you know the output size (classification = 1 word, summary = 3 sentences), cap it aggressively.

common_mistakes.title: Checking response.choices[0].text instead of .message.content
common_mistakes.explanation: The .text field is from the old /v1/completions endpoint. The chat completions response has response.choices[0].message.content. Using the wrong field returns None or AttributeError.

common_mistakes.title: Retrying on 400 errors
common_mistakes.explanation: A 400 (context_length_exceeded, invalid messages) is your code's fault — retrying won't fix it and wastes quota. Only retry 429 (rate limit) and 5xx (server errors).

recall_questions.q: What are the three required elements of a chat completions API call?
recall_questions.answer: model name, messages[] list, and API key (via client). Everything else — max_tokens, temperature, tools — is optional.

recall_questions.q: What does finish_reason='length' mean and how do you fix it?
recall_questions.answer: The model hit the max_tokens cap and was cut off mid-answer. Fix: increase max_tokens or redesign the prompt to elicit a shorter answer.

recall_questions.q: Which HTTP status codes should trigger a retry, and which should not?
recall_questions.answer: Retry: 429 (rate limit) and 5xx (server errors) with exponential backoff. Do NOT retry 400 (bad request — your bug) or 401 (bad key).

oa_questions.question: A production LLM pipeline occasionally returns None for the response text. Logs show no exceptions were raised. What are two likely causes?
oa_questions.answer: 1) Code is reading response.choices[0].text (old completions field) instead of response.choices[0].message.content — returns None silently. 2) The model returned a tool_call response instead of a text response (finish_reason='tool_calls') — message.content is None when the model wants to call a tool. Fix: check finish_reason before reading content.
oa_questions.approach: Two classic None sources: wrong field name (text vs message.content) and tool_calls finish_reason. Check finish_reason defensively.

oa_questions.question: Your pipeline's average response time doubles overnight. Token usage looks the same. What API parameter might explain this?
oa_questions.answer: max_tokens was likely removed or increased dramatically. Without a cap, the model can generate very long responses on some inputs — even if the average token count looks normal, a few long-tail responses inflate latency. Check max_tokens is set and appropriate for the task.
oa_questions.approach: Latency for LLMs scales with output tokens, not just input. Missing max_tokens is the most common cause of sudden latency spikes on text generation tasks.
